content:
First, observe that since \(OP\) is tangent to the smaller circle \(C_1\) at \(P\), we have by the tangent‐secant (or power‐of‐a‐point) theorem

\[
OP^2 \;=\; OC^2 - r^2
\;=\;(9 - r)^2 - r^2
\;=\;81 -18r,
\]

where \(r\) is the radius of \(C_1\) and \(OC=9-r\).  Hence
\[
r = \frac{81 - OP^2}{18}\,. 
\]

---

On the other hand, the ray \(OP\) meets the big circle again at \(Q\), so
\[
PQ \;=\; OQ - OP \;=\;9 - OP.
\]
Meanwhile, the tangent at \(A\) to the small circle is vertical (since \(A\) is the rightmost tangency point), and it meets the line \(OP\) at a point \(R\) with
\[
x_R=9,\quad x_P=\text{(abscissa of }P),\quad 
\]
so by similar triangles one finds
\[
QR \;=\;9\,\frac{OP - x_P}{x_P}.
\]
The condition \(PQ=QR\) becomes
\[
9 - OP \;=\;9\,\frac{OP - x_P}{x_P}
\;\Longrightarrow\;
x_P \;=\;\frac{9\,OP}{18 - OP}.
\]
But from the coordinate‐geometry or from \(x_P=(9-r)+r\cos\theta\) with \(\cos\theta=-\,\tfrac r{9-r}\) one also shows
\[
x_P \;=\;\frac{OP^2}{9-r}
\;=\;\frac{OP^2}{\,(81-OP^2)/18\,}
\;=\;\frac{18\,OP^2}{81+OP^2}\,.
\]
Hence
\[
\frac{18\,OP^2}{81+OP^2}
\;=\;
\frac{9\,OP}{18-OP}
\;\Longrightarrow\;
3\,OP^2 -36\,OP +81=0
\;\Longrightarrow\;
OP=3\quad(\text{the other root }9\text{ is extraneous}).
\]

Therefore
\[
\boxed{3}.
\]
